title: The Fundamentals: Time, CPU, Memory, and Network
summary: A mental model for profiling applications through time, CPU, memory, and network or IO, plus the optimization cycle that keeps performance work grounded.
body:
# The Fundamentals: Time, CPU, Memory, and Network

Profiling starts with a simple question: what resource is limiting the application?

Most performance problems fall into four dimensions: time, CPU, memory, and network or IO. Naming the dimension matters because each one needs a different tool and a different fix.

## Time Profiling

Time profiling looks at latency and duration. On the backend, this might mean request duration, database query time, or queue processing time. On the frontend, it might mean page load time, rendering time, or interaction delay.

Time is usually what users notice first, but it does not always explain the cause. A slow request might be slow because of CPU, memory pressure, a database query, or network latency.

## CPU Profiling

CPU profiling identifies expensive computation. Common examples include recursive algorithms, large JSON serialization, image processing, encryption, compression, parsing, and repeated rendering work.

If CPU is the bottleneck, adding indexes will not help. You need to reduce computation, cache results, move work off the main thread, or use a more efficient algorithm.

## Memory Profiling

Memory profiling looks for leaks, unnecessary allocation, and objects that stay alive longer than expected.

Memory issues can be quiet at first. The application may start fast and then degrade over hours or days. In production, this often appears as growing memory usage, more frequent garbage collection, slower responses, or process restarts.

## Network and IO Profiling

IO profiling covers anything that waits on another system: databases, APIs, disk, queues, object storage, and third-party services.

Many backend performance problems are not caused by application code running slowly. They are caused by waiting too often, fetching too much data, missing indexes, N+1 queries, or calling slow external services in the request path.

## The 80/20 Rule

Most optimization wins come from a small number of bottlenecks. Profiling helps you find that small set.

Optimizing code that only contributes 2% of total runtime will not change the user experience. Optimizing the path that contributes 60% can change everything.

## The Profiling Cycle

```txt
Define goal
   ↓
Measure current performance
   ↓
Find bottleneck
   ↓
Optimize
   ↓
Measure again
   ↓
Repeat
```

This cycle prevents performance work from becoming opinion-driven. Every change should have a before and after measurement.

## Optimizing the Wrong Part Does Not Help

If the page is slow because the hero image is too large, memoizing a React component will not fix it. If the API is slow because of an N+1 query, moving code into a helper function will not fix it. If the Node.js event loop is blocked by CPU work, adding more logging will not fix it.

Profiling makes the problem specific enough to solve.

In the next part, we will use this model to profile a Node.js backend.
